structured_choices.data_source: Longitudinal EHR data spanning 4 calendar years (Years 1–4) from a single acute-care hospital system. The dataset includes all inpatient discharges as index events, plus complete outpatient encounter histories, diagnoses (ICD codes), procedures (CPT codes), medications, lab results, vitals, and discharge summaries. Minimum 50,000 index discharges required for adequate statistical power and class balance assessment. Data is extracted at discharge and includes all clinically documented events from the prior 30 days (the lookback window).
structured_choices.label_definition: Unplanned 30-day readmission: A binary label indicating whether the patient had an inpatient admission to any service within the same hospital system within 30 calendar days of discharge, excluding planned readmissions (identified via admission reason codes, pre-scheduled procedures, or care coordination notes flagged as planned). Censoring: Patients with incomplete follow-up (e.g., transferred to external facility, lost to system follow-up, or with gaps >30 days in subsequent encounter records) are excluded from analysis to avoid label bias. A sensitivity analysis will separately evaluate performance on the subset of patients with complete 30-day follow-up confirmation.
structured_choices.split_method: Chronological (temporal) split by discharge date, stratified by discharge service and readmission status to ensure balanced class representation in each fold. Train set: Year 1 discharges (all, ~12 months). Validation set: Year 2 discharges (~12 months). Test set: Year 3–4 discharges held completely separate, with Year 3 used for final evaluation and Year 4 reserved for a secondary robustness check on temporal drift. This split prevents leakage from future coding practices, shifts in discharge criteria, or systematic changes in readmission rates over time. It also reflects the deployment scenario: a model trained on historical data must generalize to future, unseen discharge cohorts.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set (Year 1 discharges). Fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets to simulate prospective deployment. Specifically: (1) continuous features (lab values, vitals, LOS) are standardized using training-set mean and variance; (2) categorical features (diagnosis codes, procedures, discharge service) are one-hot or target-encoded using only training-set observed categories—test-set categories not seen in training are mapped to an 'other' category; (3) missing values are imputed using training-set medians or modes. No statistics from validation or test data inform any preprocessing step.
structured_choices.features: Two feature sets are constructed: (A) Sequence model input: Temporally ordered list of events (encounters, diagnoses, procedures, medications, lab results, vitals) from the 30-day lookback window, with event timestamps and type identifiers. Raw events are embedded (e.g., diagnosis codes via pre-trained clinical word embeddings or one-hot encoding, lab values normalized) and fed into an LSTM or Transformer. (B) Baseline aggregated features: Summary statistics computed over the same 30-day window: (i) encounter frequency (count of outpatient visits, ED encounters); (ii) length of stay (index admission LOS); (iii) comorbidity burden (Elixhauser or Charlson index computed from diagnoses in prior 30 days); (iv) medication count; (v) lab abnormality count (e.g., out-of-range values); (vi) discharge service; (vii) age, sex. The baseline feature set is intentionally comparable in scope—same lookback window, same information source—but lacks temporal structure. Both sets exclude future information (no post-discharge events, no labels).
structured_choices.model: Primary model: Sequence model (LSTM with attention or Transformer encoder) that consumes temporally ordered events from the 30-day lookback window. Architecture: embedding layer for event types and values, bidirectional LSTM (2 layers, 64–128 hidden units) or Transformer encoder (4 layers, 8 attention heads), followed by global average pooling and a 2-layer dense network (128, 64 units, ReLU, dropout=0.3) terminating in a sigmoid output layer for binary classification. Hyperparameters (embedding dim, hidden size, dropout, learning rate, batch size) are tuned via random search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set (see Validation approach).
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the aggregated feature set (B above). Logistic regression is a strong, interpretable baseline well-suited to tabular features and ensures the comparison is not about model class but specifically about the value of temporal sequence information. To ensure equal tuning effort: (1) the baseline receives the same hyperparameter optimization budget (e.g., 50 random trials with cross-validation on the training set); (2) L2 regularization strength is tuned on validation set performance; (3) class weights are adjusted on the training set to account for any class imbalance. An alternative secondary baseline (Random Forest on aggregated features) may also be trained to check whether the improvement is specific to deep learning or reflects the information content of sequences.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: AUROC measures discrimination across all classification thresholds, which is clinically appropriate because care managers need to rank patients by risk and decide how many high-risk cases to allocate resources to. AUROC is threshold-independent and robust to class imbalance (readmission prevalence typically ~15–20% in real data). AUROC also does not require choosing an arbitrary operating point a priori, allowing the clinical team to set their preferred sensitivity–specificity trade-off post-hoc based on resource constraints.
secondary_metrics: Average Precision (area under the precision–recall curve): Relevant given potential class imbalance; emphasizes performance on the minority (readmitted) class.

Calibration: Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and calibration plots (predicted probability vs. observed frequency in deciles): Clinicians must trust risk scores to allocate interventions, so miscalibrated models could harm decision-making even if discrimination is high.
Sensitivity and specificity at clinically plausible thresholds (e.g., top 20% risk, top 10% risk): Allows reporting of actionable performance metrics tied to actual deployment thresholds.
Subgroup performance: AUROC and calibration stratified by discharge service, age group, and comorbidity burden to detect disparate performance that might flag confounding or fairness concerns.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and model selection use the validation set (Year 2 discharges) only. The sequence model and baseline are each trained on the full training set and evaluated on the validation set. The hyperparameter configuration (and, if multiple models are trained, the final model) that achieves the highest AUROC on the validation set is selected. The test set (Year 3–4 discharges) is never touched during tuning or selection; it is used only once, at the very end, to report final performance and compute confidence intervals.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is partitioned into Year 3 (primary test set, ~12 months of discharges) and Year 4 (secondary robustness test, ~12 months). The Year 3 test set is used to compute the final reported metrics (AUROC, AUPRC, calibration, subgroup performance). The Year 4 test set is used separately to assess temporal generalization: if performance degrades significantly from Year 3 to Year 4, it signals non-stationarity or drift that should be flagged in the analysis. Neither test set is used during training or validation. Confidence intervals (95%, via bootstrap or cross-fold resampling on test predictions) are computed for all primary and secondary metrics to quantify uncertainty.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confounds: (1) Recency / utilization intensity: The model may gain predictive power simply from the frequency of recent encounters (a proxy for severity) rather than from temporal structure. Control: A feature ablation study removes encounter frequency from the baseline and evaluates performance. If baseline AUROC drops substantially, recency was driving it; if sequence model AUROC remains high, the model is capturing additional signal. (2) Length of stay and discharge service: Systematically associated with readmission risk and may confound the comparison if one model better captures LOS or service effects. Control: Both models receive LOS and discharge service as inputs. Subgroup stratified analysis is reported by discharge service to verify that gains are not localized to specific services. (3) Censoring and label bias: Unplanned vs. planned readmissions are conflated. Control: Planned readmissions are explicitly excluded during label construction via admission reason codes. A sensitivity analysis repeats evaluation on a more conservative label set (excluding borderline cases where planned vs. unplanned is ambiguous) to test robustness. (4) External follow-up: Patients receiving care outside the health system are not observed. Control: Acknowledged as a limitation; the experiment does not claim to predict true 30-day readmission risk in the population but rather readmission within the observed health system. Any future deployment must account for this in interpretation.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Aggregated Features for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Preparation

We will use longitudinal EHR data spanning 4 calendar years (Years 1–4) from a single acute-care hospital system, comprising all inpatient discharges as index events. Each discharge record is enriched with complete clinical histories from the prior 30 days: diagnoses (ICD codes), procedures (CPT codes), medications, lab results, vitals, encounter types, and timestamps. We require a minimum of 50,000 index discharges to support robust model training and reliable subgroup stratification. All data are de-identified and extracted at the time of discharge; no post-discharge information is included in the feature engineering window.

### 2. Label Definition and Censoring

The outcome is binary: **unplanned 30-day readmission**. We define readmission as any inpatient admission to the same health system within 30 calendar days of discharge. Planned readmissions—identified via structured admission reason codes, pre-scheduled procedures, or care coordination notes flagged as 'planned'—are excluded to focus on avoidable readmissions. We exclude patients with incomplete 30-day follow-up (e.g., transferred out of system, lost to follow-up) to avoid label bias, and we conduct a sensitivity analysis on the subset with confirmed complete follow-up.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy

To prevent leakage from future practice patterns, coding changes, and patient overlap, we employ a **chronological split stratified by discharge service and readmission status**:

- **Training set:** Year 1 discharges (~12 months, ~25,000 discharges)
- **Validation set:** Year 2 discharges (~12 months, ~12,500 discharges)
- **Test set:** Year 3 discharges (primary, ~12,500 discharges) and Year 4 discharges (secondary robustness check, ~12,500 discharges)

This split mirrors the deployment scenario: the model is trained on historical data and must generalize to future, unseen cohorts. Stratification ensures balanced class representation across splits and discharge services, preventing the model from learning spurious associations tied to a particular year or service mix.

### 4. Feature Engineering

**Sequence model input (primary):** Temporally ordered events from the 30-day lookback window, including encounter types, diagnoses, procedures, medications, lab measurements, and vitals, each tagged with a timestamp. Events are embedded (e.g., diagnosis codes via clinical embeddings or one-hot encoding) and normalized (lab values standardized using training-set statistics). This preserves temporal structure and clinical causality.

**Baseline aggregated features:** Summary statistics over the same 30-day window:
- Encounter frequency (count of outpatient visits, ED encounters)
- Length of stay (index admission LOS)
- Comorbidity burden (Elixhauser or Charlson index)
- Medication and lab abnormality counts
- Discharge service, age, sex

Both feature sets draw from identical information sources and the same temporal window, ensuring a fair comparison of sequence structure vs. aggregation.

**Preprocessing fit point:** All scalers, encoders, and imputation strategies are fit on the training set only. Fitted transformers are applied identically to validation and test sets, simulating prospective deployment and preventing data leakage.

### 5. Model and Baseline

**Primary model:** A sequence model (bidirectional LSTM with attention or Transformer encoder) that consumes temporally ordered events. Architecture: embedding layer → LSTM/Transformer encoder (2–4 layers, 64–128 hidden units) → global pooling → dense layers (128, 64 units) → sigmoid output. Hyperparameters are tuned via random search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set (50 trials).

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on aggregated features, with identical hyperparameter tuning effort (L2 regularization strength tuned on validation set; class weights adjusted for imbalance). This baseline is strong, interpretable, and ensures the comparison isolates the value of temporal sequencing, not model sophistication.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). AUROC measures discrimination across all thresholds, is robust to class imbalance, and does not require pre-specifying an operating point. Clinicians can then choose their preferred sensitivity–specificity trade-off based on available resources.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Average Precision (AUPRC): Emphasizes minority class (readmitted) performance.
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE): Clinicians trust risk scores only if well-calibrated; miscalibration can harm decision-making.
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically meaningful thresholds (e.g., top 20%, top 10% risk).
- Subgroup performance (by discharge service, age, comorbidity burden) to detect disparities or confounding.

### 7. Validation and Model Selection

Both the sequence model and baseline are trained on the full training set (Year 1) and evaluated on the validation set (Year 2). The model configuration achieving the highest AUROC on the validation set is selected. **The test set is never touched during tuning or selection.** Final performance is reported on Year 3 (primary test set) with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap). Year 4 is evaluated separately to assess temporal drift; significant performance degradation signals non-stationarity requiring monitoring in production.

### 8. Controlling for Confounds

We address known confounds as follows:

- **Recency / utilization intensity:** Both models receive encounter frequency; we also conduct ablation studies (removing frequency from the baseline) to test whether gains are driven by temporal structure or simply by capturing recent activity.
- **Length of stay and service effects:** Both models include LOS and discharge service. Stratified subgroup analysis ensures gains are not localized to specific services.
- **Label bias (planned vs. unplanned):** Planned readmissions are explicitly excluded via structured codes. A sensitivity analysis uses a conservative label definition to test robustness.
- **External follow-up:** Acknowledged limitation; predictions reflect readmission within the observed health system, and deployment interpretations must account for this.

### 9. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is supported if the sequence model achieves statistically significant higher AUROC on the test set compared to the baseline (difference >0.02 with non-overlapping 95% CIs, or p < 0.05 on a one-sided Mann–Whitney U test on out-of-sample predictions). Gains must persist in subgroup analysis and when controlling for encounter frequency, confirming that the model captures clinical signal beyond recency or utilization intensity.
